Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard binary package sourced from a `.deb` file hosted on GitHub Releases. The `source_x86_64` array references a direct HTTPS URL to the release artifact, which is a common and acceptable practice for AUR -bin packages. The checksum is explicitly provided and matches the expected hash for the release, ensuring integrity. No `pkgver()` function is defined, so no dynamic version calculation occurs at parse time.

There is no top-level command substitution, code execution, or network activity outside of the declared `source` fetch, which is expected during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. The `package()` function is irrelevant for this gate as it is not executed under `--noprepare`. The rest of the metadata (depends, provides, conflicts, etc.) consists of static declarations with no side effects. Running the allowed commands will safely download the specified `.deb` archive without executing any arbitrary or hidden code.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: No harmful code in sourcing or source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: No harmful code in sourcing or source fetch[/]

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, project-graph-nightly-bin.install...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves a prebuilt Debian package (.deb) from the project's official GitHub releases page and extracts its contents using standard tar commands. The source URL is a fixed release asset from the official repository, and the SHA-256 checksum is provided and matches the expected integrity verification standard. No dynamic or untrusted downloads, obfuscated code, or malicious operations (e.g., data exfiltration, remote execution) are present.

The package installation procedure uses a straightforward `tar` extraction into the build directory (`${pkgdir}`), which is a common and safe method when handling binary blobs. The dependencies and metadata are appropriate for a GUI application using GTK and WebKit. While the use of a binary-only package from GitHub prevents source review of the actual application, this is a trust decision common in `-bin` packages and not indicative of a supply-chain compromise in the AUR packaging itself.

The overall structure follows standard AUR packaging practices for `-bin` packages. No evidence of injected malicious code, unexpected network operations, or unsafe scripting.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package from official source</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing project-graph-nightly-bin.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package from official source
LLM auditresponse for project-graph-nightly-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.install` script contains only standard post-installation, post-upgrade, and post-removal hooks commonly used in Arch Linux packages. These functions call `gtk-update-icon-cache` and `update-desktop-database` to refresh system caches related to application icons and desktop entries, which is a routine and expected operation when installing or removing desktop applications.

There are no suspicious commands such as `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, or `exec`, and no evidence of unauthorized network activity, file exfiltration, or execution of downloaded code. The operations are limited to cache updates in standard system paths under `/usr/share`, which aligns with the legitimate purpose of ensuring desktop integration for the installed application.

No obfuscated code or unexpected system modifications are present. All actions are transparent, minimal, and consistent with Arch packaging best practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard cache updates, no malicious activity detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for md5sums:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file contains only md5sums for various files included in the package, such as a binary, desktop entry, and icon files. These checksums are used to verify the integrity of the corresponding files during the package build process. There is no executable code, network activity, or file operations in this file. The use of md5sums, while less secure than sha256, is a standard practice in Arch Linux packaging, especially for binary packages where reproducibility may not be feasible. The checksums cover only local package files and do not reference external downloads or scripts. This file does not introduce any risk of code execution, data exfiltration, or system modification.
</details>
<summary>
Checksums only, no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing src/control, src/usr/share/applications/Project Graph.desktop...
+ Reviewed src/md5sums. Status: SAFE -- Checksums only, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for Project Graph.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.desktop` file is a standard desktop entry configuration used to integrate the application into the system's application menu and MIME type handling. It declares standard fields such as `Name`, `Comment`, `Exec`, `Icon`, and `Categories`, all of which are consistent with desktop entry specifications and the stated purpose of the application (a diagram creator).

The `Exec=project-graph` line invokes the application binary in a standard way, and `StartupWMClass=project-graph` ensures proper window management. The `MimeType` entry associates the application with custom MIME types (`application/vnd.project-graph` and `x-scheme-handler/prg`), which is typical for applications that handle custom file formats or URL schemes. There are no suspicious commands, encoded payloads, or network operations.

No potentially harmful behaviors such as code execution, data exfiltration, or unexpected system modifications are present. The file contains no scripts or logic—only declarative metadata. As such, it poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry file, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing src/control...
+ Reviewed src/usr/share/applications/Project Graph.desktop. Status: SAFE -- Standard desktop entry file, no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for control:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is a Debian control file, which defines metadata for a package named "project-graph". It specifies basic package information such as version, architecture, dependencies, and description. There is no executable code, script, or command injection present.

The declared dependencies (libwebkit2gtk-4.1-0, libgtk-3-0) are standard libraries for GUI applications on Linux and are consistent with a graphical diagramming tool. The description indicates the software is a simple tool for creating topology diagrams, which aligns with the stated functionality.

No network operations, file manipulations, or suspicious commands are present in this file. It contains only declarative metadata typical of a Debian package, posing no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Metadata-only file, no executable content.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a single source entry that downloads a `.deb` file from the project's own GitHub releases over HTTPS. The checksum is provided as a fixed SHA256. There are no command substitutions, function calls, or other executable code at the global scope or in `pkgver()` (which is not defined). The `package()` function is present but is not executed by `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. No dangerous operations (e.g., `curl | bash`, base64 decoding, unexpected network requests) are triggered by the source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing. The download target is the project's official release URL, which is standard and expected.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>

<details>
This is a standard Arch Linux `.install` script that performs routine post-installation tasks: updating the GTK icon cache and desktop database. These commands (`gtk-update-icon-cache`, `update-desktop-database`) are expected for any package that ships icons or `.desktop` files. There are no network operations, obfuscated code, unusual system modifications, or any behavior that deviates from normal packaging practices. The script does exactly what it is supposed to do without any indicators of a supply-chain attack.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard AUR package that downloads a binary release from the official upstream GitHub repository (`https://github.com/LiRenTech/project-graph/releases/download/nightly/...`) with a pinned SHA256 checksum. The `package()` function appears to extract the `.deb` archive's data tarball, though it does so without first extracting the `.deb` container – this is likely a packaging error (the `data.tar.gz` would not exist directly) but not a security concern. No obfuscated code, unexpected network requests, or dangerous commands are present. The provided `.install` file is referenced but its content is not part of this analysis; the PKGBUILD itself contains no malicious elements.
</details>
<summary>Standard binary package from official upstream source.</summary>
</security_assessment>
